visual_dna.ethnicity: 1st Century Jewish, Galilean fisherman from Bethsaida
visual_dna.skin_tone: deeply tanned and weathered from years on the Sea of Galilee — sun and wind
visual_dna.hair_color: dark brown, greying at the temples — he is older than most of the disciples
visual_dna.eye_color: brown — wide and reactive, his face shows everything he feels
visual_dna.height_build: stocky, broad-shouldered, powerful from years hauling nets — the biggest man in the group
distinguishing_marks: large calloused hands — fisherman's hands, not scholar's hands
his face broadcasts every emotion — he cannot hide anything
impulsive posture — always slightly leaning forward, ready to speak or act
the kind of physical presence that fills a room without trying

wardrobe_by_arc.fishing_call: working fisherman's tunic, damp and salt-stained, possibly knee-length for boat work
wardrobe_by_arc.ministry: simple traveling robe, worn sandals — he left the boat without packing
wardrobe_by_arc.transfiguration: travel robe, dusty from mountain climb, disheveled
wardrobe_by_arc.denial: outer cloak wrapped tight against the cold night of the high priest's courtyard

voice_profile.register: rough loud baritone — a man used to calling across water
voice_profile.pace: fast — he speaks before he finishes thinking
voice_profile.accent_note: thick Galilean accent — so distinctive that a servant girl identifies him by it at the denial (Luke 22:59)
speech_mannerisms: declarations: 'You are the Messiah of God' — maximum confidence
volunteers first: 'I will build three shelters' (at Transfiguration)
'Even if all fall away, I will not' — the promise he cannot keep
the denial: three times, each one faster than the last
voice_profile.notes: His Galilean accent is historically documented in the denial scene. This is a casting and voice coaching note.

emotional_arc.opening: Proud Galilean fisherman — successful business, boat, partners, family. Then Jesus says 'Follow me.' He leaves everything.
emotional_arc.midpoint: Inner circle — he sees the Transfiguration, he makes the great confession. He feels closest to Jesus when he is most about to fail.
emotional_arc.climax: The courtyard denial — three times, the last time with an oath. Then the rooster. Then Jesus turns and looks at him.
emotional_arc.resolution: He went outside and wept bitterly. Luke 24 — he runs to the tomb. He is being restored.

evangelical_baptist.notes: The disciple who failed and was restored — core to salvation narrative, grace, redemption
evangelical_baptist.marketing_angle: He promised everything. Failed completely. Got back up. That's the whole story.

catholic.notes: The first pope — his primacy, his failure, his restoration all matter theologically
catholic.marketing_angle: The first pope — flawed, forgiven, and given the keys anyway. His story is the Church's story.

black_church.notes: The imperfect vessel restored — his failure and restoration is a testimony
black_church.marketing_angle: God doesn't use perfect people. Peter proves it.

hispanic_christian.notes: The man of big heart and big failure — restoration is central
hispanic_christian.marketing_angle: El hombre que prometió todo y falló — y fue restaurado de todos modos

secular_curious.notes: The most relatable disciple — he gets it wrong constantly, loudly, in front of everyone
secular_curious.marketing_angle: He was the loudest, most confident one. He failed more publicly than anyone else. His story is the most human.

youth_gen_z.notes: The one who talks too big and fails — and is not defined by it
youth_gen_z.marketing_angle: He said the worst thing you can say and still came back. That's the whole point.

jewish_american.notes: The Galilean fisherman — his world is accurately the world of 1st century Jewish Galilee
jewish_american.marketing_angle: A Galilean fisherman — the fishing world of the Sea of Galilee, period-accurate

women.notes: Less directly resonant but his failure vs the women's faithfulness at the cross is a noted contrast
women.marketing_angle: The women stayed at the cross. He ran. Luke doesn't hide that.

men.notes: The man who failed publicly and came back — the most direct male redemption arc in the gospels
men.marketing_angle: He promised everything and failed. Wept alone. Then ran to the empty tomb. Men know this arc.

audience_targeting.modern_parallel: The guy who talks too big, fails publicly, weeps alone, and gets back up anyway
audience_targeting.modern_parallel_detail: Not a tragic figure. A restored one. Luke 22:32 — Jesus says 'when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers.' The restoration is already spoken before the failure happens.

ai_generation.consistency_seed_prompt: 1st century Jewish fisherman man age 30-35, deeply tanned sea-weathered skin, dark brown hair greying at temples, full medium beard, broad stocky powerful build, large calloused hands, simple working or traveling tunic, Sea of Galilee or Galilean landscape, cinematic photoreal, natural daylight, 8k, film grain
ai_generation.negative_prompt: slim build, fine clothing, groomed aristocratic hair, cartoonish, European features
ai_generation.midjourney_style_params: cinematic photoreal, natural daylight, warm Galilean tones, authentic working-class look
ai_generation.firefly_prompt: Photorealistic portrait of a 1st century Jewish Galilean fisherman, age 30-35, deeply tanned sea-weathered skin, dark brown hair with grey temples, full medium beard, broad stocky powerful build, large rough hands, simple worn tunic, Sea of Galilee background with fishing boats, natural warm daylight, authentic working-man appearance
reference_films: The Chosen TV series — Jonathan Roumie cast but look at Shahar Isaac as Peter for physicality
The Passion of the Christ (2004) — disciples present at passion
ai_generation.color_grading_notes: Galilee scenes: warm golden sea-light. Denial scene: cold blue-grey night, courtyard fire casting orange on his face.

production_notes: The look Jesus gives Peter after the denial (Luke 22:61) — 'the Lord turned and looked straight at Peter' — is one of the greatest single shots in the film. No words. Just the look.
